mappings.rationaleAI: The Islamic Golden Age's intellectual production across mathematics, sciences, philosophy, theology, law, and arts during the Abbasid caliphate directly addresses the development of Islamic religion and culture (A.4), Islamic theology and philosophy (B.4), and Islamic art and iconography (C.4).

Mecca as Islam's holiest city, birthplace of Muhammad and Islam, housing the Kaaba and Masjid al-Haram, directly addresses the pre-Islamic setting in Arabia (A.1), the origin of Islam (A.2), the pilgrimage to Mecca as one of the Five Pillars (C.3.a), and sacred places (C.3.b).

Muhammad's life, revelations, Hijra to Medina, and establishment of Islamic law directly address the origin of Islam (A.2). The Quran as revelation connects to the Qur'an (B.1) and the Hadith (B.2). His prophetic role addresses doctrines concerning Muhammad and prophecy (C.1.e). His establishment of Shari'ah relates to law and jurisprudence (C.3.d).

Islam as a comprehensive article covering six articles of faith, five pillars, Sunni-Shia division, and prophetic tradition addresses the entire scope of Section 828: history (A), intellectual expressions (B), and beliefs, practices, and institutions (C).

Rumi as a Sufi poet and mystic, founder of the Mevlevi Order, whose doctrine of fana (annihilation of ego in divine love) bridges Persian and Turkish traditions, directly addresses the mystical path: Sufism (B.5).

Ali as fourth Rashidun caliph and first Imam of Shia Islam, whose succession dispute enabled the Umayyad Caliphate, directly addresses the foundations of the Islamic community (A.3) and Shi'ism (C.2.d).
Jesus as a revered prophet in Islam, regarded as one of the predecessors to Muhammad, connects to doctrines concerning prophecy (C.1.e).
Islamic Kalam and Arabic-Persian philosophical traditions directly relate to Islamic theology and philosophy (B.4).
The Middle Ages' Islamic expansion and consolidation connect to the Middle Ages of Islam covering theology, law, and culture (A.5).
Islamic hadith transmission as a major oral tradition directly relates to the Hadith: the oral tradition (B.2).
Al-Khwarizmi's contributions to algebra and mathematics at the House of Wisdom in Baghdad exemplify the cultural life and scientific achievements during the Abbasid caliphate (A.4).

The Quran as God's verbatim revelation comprising 114 suras covering theology, ethics, law, and eschatology directly addresses the Qur'an (B.1). Its function as primary source of Islamic law relates to Shari'ah (C.3.d).
Suleiman's synthesis of sultanic and Sharia legal codes connects to the Middle Ages of Islam in law (A.5) and Shari'ah (C.3.d). His patronage of Ottoman architecture relates to Islamic art (C.4).
Akbar's religious pluralism and Din-i Ilahi connecting Islam with Hinduism and Zoroastrianism relate to the Middle Ages of Islam (A.5) and variations within the orthodox community (C.2).
Prayer as communicative act directed toward God, with fixed liturgical forms, directly addresses the five daily prayers as one of the Five Pillars (C.3.a).
Abraham as a prophet in Islam in the chain from Adam to Muhammad connects to doctrines concerning prophecy (C.1.e).
Law as enforceable rules including religious law systems connects to Islamic law: Shari'ah, fiqh (B.3) and the Shari'ah and schools of law (C.3.d).
